coredata.dc:title: A new fluorescence “turn on” nanoplatform based on conjugated polymer nanoparticles and Au nanorods for the detection of hyaluronidase
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qiu-bo
Fu, Wen
Ma, Rui-jian
Lu, Qian
Liu, Zhao-e
Yao, Jin-shui
Liu, Qinze
Zhang, Xian
Zhao, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Hyaluronidase (HAase) is a momentous hydrolase related to many physiological and pathological processes. Therefore, it is urgent to establish a simple and accurate method for HAase detection. The combination of conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs) and Au nanorods (AuNRs) provides an innovative method for platform design. In this work, a fluorescence platform was established for quantitative detection of HAase. Combined CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs using hyaluronic acid (HA) as the core by electrostatic adsorption, a novel “turn on” nanoplatform (CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs) was established. The fluorescence of CPNs-NH2 will be quenched because of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs. In the presence of HAase, AuNRs can be effectively decomposed, leading to the recovery of CPNs-NH2 fluorescence. The lower limit of detection of CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs is obtained of 0.003 U/mL and the linear detection range is achieved within 1–45 U/mL. Furthermore, CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs was applied in practice to the determination of HAase with high selectivity and sensitivity. With its superior performance, it was successfully applied to HAase quantification in biological environment. The experimental results show that CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs is of great significance for the diagnosis of HAase related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CPNs
AuNRs
Fluorescence platform
Hyaluronidase

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Labeling test of HAase in vitro
The design and optimization of the platform (CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)The absorption (Ⅰ) and fluorescence emission spectra (Ⅱ) of CPNs-NH2 solution (25 μg/mL). The absorption (Ⅳ) and fluorescence emission spectra (Ⅲ) of AuNRs solution (4.4 μM). (b)Fluorescence lifetime curve of CPNs-NH2 (1 μg/mL) with the addition of AuNRs (0 μM; 0.22 μM; 0.44 μM; 2 μM) and CPNs-NH2-HA@AuNRs (0.22 μM; 0.44 μM; 2 μM). AuNRs solution (4.4 μM) and CPNs-NH2 solution (25 μg/mL) under common light (c) (d) and ultraviolet light (e) (f), respectively.

TEM images. Nanoparticles prepared by PBOC (25 μg/mL) and different concentrations of PEI (5 μg/mL (a); 10 μg/mL (b); 15 μg/mL (c). Particle size distribution of CPNs-NH2 (1 μg/mL) in the different concentrations ratio of PBOC and PEI (5:1 (d); 5:2 (e); 5:3 (f)) by DLS. AuNRs (g) and CPNs-NH2-HA @AuNRs of different concentrations of HA (h:0.3 μg/mL; i:0.5 μg/mL; j: 1 μg/mL).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CPNs-NH2 (1 μg/mL) with the addition of different concentrations of AuNRs (0–2 μM) (a) and HA (0∼5.5 μg/mL) (b) (Inset: the fluorescence trend at 545 nm). The fluorescence of CPNs-NH2 with the addition of different concentrations of HA (0–5 μg/mL) at the AuNRs concentrations of 0.22 μM, (c); 0.44 μM (e) and 2 μM (g), respectively; (Inset: the fluorescence trend at 545 nm). And their Zeta potential change of CPNs-NH2/AuNRs (1 μg/mL/0.22 μM) (d); (1 μg/mL/0.44 μM) (f) and (1 μg/mL/2 μM) (h) with the addition of different concentrations of HA (0–5 μg/mL).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs with the addition of different concentrations of HAase (0–75 U/mL) (a) (Inset: the fluorescence trend at 545 nm); A linear relationship between 1–45 U/mL at 545 nm (b). Effect of different ions and coexisting compounds on the fluorescence intensity for sensing HAase (c) (NaCl, KCl, 100 mM; Glu, GSH, 500 μM; lysozyme, GOD, HRP, 90 U/mL; BSA, 10 mg/mL; HAase, 70 U/mL). F1–F0 is the difference of the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe in the presence and absence of a species. (Inset: its fluorescence emission spectra). The viability of HeLa cells incubated with different concentrations of CPNs-NH2 (0 μg/mL∼10 μg/mL) (d) and different concentrations of AuNRs (0 μM–2.5 μM) in the presence of CPNs-NH2 (2 μg/mL) and HA (2 μg/mL) (e).
Labeling experiment results of HAase in actual samples (mouse serum and plasma (a); human serum and plasma (b)).
The preparation process and mechanism of fluorescence nanoplatform.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The concentration of CPNs-NH2 was 1 μg/mL. The Zeta potential was the result of different concentrations of AuNRs and HA in CPNs-NH2 solution. The quenching efficiency was calculated by F/F0. F0 and F were the fluorescent intensity of CPNs-NH2 before and after at the isoelectric point adding HA. “---” means that HA has no obvious quenching effect on the fluorescence of CPNs-NH2.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparing with other reported fluorescence detection methods of HAase.

head.ce:title: A new fluorescence “turn on” nanoplatform based on conjugated polymer nanoparticles and Au nanorods for the detection of hyaluronidase

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan 250353, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qilu Hospital of Shandong University, Jinan 250012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Fundamental Sciences and Frontiers, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 610054, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Fundamental Sciences and Frontiers
University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Fundamental Sciences and Frontiers, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu 610054, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hyaluronidase (HAase) is a momentous hydrolase related to many physiological and pathological processes. Therefore, it is urgent to establish a simple and accurate method for HAase detection. The combination of conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs) and Au nanorods (AuNRs) provides an innovative method for platform design. In this work, a fluorescence platform was established for quantitative detection of HAase. Combined CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs using hyaluronic acid (HA) as the core by electrostatic adsorption, a novel “turn on” nanoplatform (CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs) was established. The fluorescence of CPNs-NH2 will be quenched because of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs. In the presence of HAase, AuNRs can be effectively decomposed, leading to the recovery of CPNs-NH2 fluorescence. The lower limit of detection of CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs is obtained of 0.003 U/mL and the linear detection range is achieved within 1–45 U/mL. Furthermore, CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs was applied in practice to the determination of HAase with high selectivity and sensitivity. With its superior performance, it was successfully applied to HAase quantification in biological environment. The experimental results show that CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs is of great significance for the diagnosis of HAase related diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Hyaluronic acid (HA) is composed of repeating units of N-acetylglucosamine and glucuronidase [1]. It is the major component of the natural extracellular matrix of almost mammalian connective tissues [2]. HA has high negative charges and is involved in many pathological and physiological processes such as fertilization, embryogenesis, inflammation, wound healing, tissue maintenance and tumor growth. Hyaluronidase (HAase) is an extracellular matrix digestive glycosidase and can specifically digest HA into small segments [3]. Therefore, it plays an important role in the degradation of HA and the process of tumor cell metastasis. In recent years, many studies have shown that the activity of HAase is related to many processes such as embryogenesis, inflammation and overexpression of some cancers (such as bladder, or prostate) [4]. Therefore, as a tumor marker, the activity of HAase in cells has been widely concerned.

At present, various methods have been proposed to detect the activity of HAase. However, the traditional methods, such as viscosimetry [5], colorimetry [6], turbidimetry [7], either lack sensitivity and selectivity, or need a lot of time [8]. Immunoassay is a sensitive and selective method, but it needs special and expensive reagents [9]. The fluorescence method [10] has been proved to be the one of the most useful analytical tools in biological analysis and imaging. Nanoplatform is usually used in fluorescence detection of HAase [11,12], and their sensitivity (10−1–10−2 U/mL) still need to be improved. The content of HA in the sample is usually very little [13,14], which requires us to design a new fluorescence method to improve the sensitivity.

Conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs) have excellent optical properties and extended π-conjugated structure to obtain fluorescence signal amplification response [15,16]. Because of its advantages including good water solubility and easy surface modification, it has a wide application prospect in biomedical applications such as biosensor, intracellular imaging and drug delivery [17,18]. Au nanorods (AuNRs) are rod-shaped Au nanoparticles, which have more unique photoelectric properties than spherical Au colloids, such as tunable aspect ratio, superior spectral bandwidth and controllable size [19,20]. Therefore, it has developed rapidly in the field of optical imaging and tumor treatment in recent years [21,22]. However, the combination of CPNs and AuNRs for fluorescence imaging has not been reported. Both of combination is expected to obtain a stable and good fluorescence platform. Moreover, the nanoplatform with the high selectivity was designed by to the specificity of HAase to HA.

Herein, we designed a novel fluorescence platform with CPNs as fluorescence cores, HA as linker and AuNRs as quenching agents. Amino-conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPNs-NH2) were prepared by nano-precipitation method with a conjugated polymer (PBOC) designed by our group and polyethyleneimine (PEI) (Scheme 1 ). According to the test, CPNs-NH2 and HA, AuNRs were self-assembled into nanoplatform (CPNs-NH2-HA@AuNRs) due to electrostatic adsorption. The fluorescence of CPNs-NH2 was quenched by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [23–25]. With the introduction of HAase, HA was degraded into small fragments, which resulted in the release of CPNs-NH2 and fluorescence recovery. The experimental results showed that the fluorescent platform has high selectivity and sensitivity to HAase.

The details of materials and methods are described in supporting information. CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs were prepared according to previous reports, and the detailed process is recorded in the supporting information [16,20].

According to our design, HA with negative charges will self-assemble with CPNs-NH2 with positive charges to get CPNs-NH2-HA. Subsequently, HA on the surface of CPNs-NH2 will continue to self-assemble with the added AuNRs to obtain the nanoplatform. In order to get the best fluorescence nanoplatform, three steps of experiments were done and carefully studied.

First step (The influence of AuNRs to CPNs-NH2 ): AuNRs solution of different concentrations (0–1.98 μM) were added to CPNs-NH2 solution (1 μg/mL). After incubation for 15 min, fluorescence test was carried out. The optimal concentration (0.22 μM; 0.44 μM; 2 μM) of AuNRs solution was determined according to the fluorescence trend. The details were described in the latter part.

Second step (The influence of HA to CPNs-NH2 ): HA solution of different concentrations (0–5.5 μg/mL) were added to CPNs-NH2 solution to study the self-assembly process of CPNs-NH2 and HA. The mixed solution was stirred well and reacted in the dark for 20 min, then the fluorescence test was carried out.

Third step (Establishment of CPNs-NH2-HA@AuNRs): HA solutions of different concentrations (0–5 μg/mL) were added to CPNs-NH2 solution and incubated for 20 min. Then adding AuNRs solution (0.22 μM; 0.44 μM; 2 μM) to the mixed solution, and the fluorescence and Zeta potential were measured to determine the optimal project.

The optimized fluorescence platform was obtained according to the above process. Then, different concentrations of HAase solution (0–75 U/mL) were added to the platform for reaction for 20 min, and then fluorescence tests were carried out.

To evaluate the selectivity of the platform, different interfering substances, including glutathione (GSH; 500 μM), glucose (Glu; 500 μM), bovine serum albumin (BSA; 10 mg/mL), lysozyme (90 U/mL), glucose oxidase (GOD; 90 U/mL), horseradish peroxidase (HRP; 90 U/mL), NaCl (100 mM); KCl (100 mM) and HAase (70 U/mL) were employed. The parameters of the fluorescent test are the same as those of the previous test settings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Labeling test of HAase in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs solution was added to the purchased mouse serum and plasma and incubated at room temperature for 10 min, respectively. Then, the HAase solutions with different concentrations were allowed to stand for 30 min for the fluorescence test. Human serum and plasma were diluted to different concentrations of HAase samples (human serum: 5.1 U/mL and 10.4 U/mL; human plasma: 6.3 U/mL and 13.6 U/mL), and carried out the labeling experiments. The parameters of the fluorescent test are the same as the previous test settings.

AuNRs is a kind of promising metal material with a tunable aspect ratio, which results in the change of UV absorption peak. Based on this, we have prepared AuNRs with a small aspect ratio and a suitable UV absorption range. This can be benefit to generate FRET with CPNs-NH2. AuNRs solution is blue-black under natural light (Fig. 1b), and there is no obvious fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1d). In Fig. 1a-Ⅳ, one can find that AuNRs have an obvious absorption peak at 670 nm. Fig. 1a-Ⅲ showed that there was no obvious fluorescence emission peak for AuNRs. TEM image (Fig. 2g) shows that AuNRs are mainly rod-shaped, and the aspect ratio is mostly about 2 (Length: 30 nm, Width: 15 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design and optimization of the platform (CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs)
ce:section.ce:para: In order to create a highly responsive platform for HAase detection, we designed a fluorescent “turn off-on” nanoplatform based on FRET. Considering that HA has negative charges and CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs have positive charges, which may generate the self-assemble using HA as the core, CPNs-NH2 as the donor and AuNRs as the receptor. They are closely linked to each other to produce FRET, which can quench the fluorescence of CPNs-NH2. The introduction of HA can not only make the two nanomaterials closely linked to provide the opportunity for FRET, but also make the nanoplatform have a specific response to HAase. The key of the nanoplatform lies in the formation of the interaction between the different components. That is important to find the best concentration ratio. Therefore, we did a series of experiments to build an ideal nanoplatform.

Fig. 1a-Ⅲ displayed that AuNRs have a wide range of absorption from 200 nm to 800 nm, and the fluorescence emission range of CPNs-NH2 was from 450 nm to 650 nm (Fig. 1a-Ⅱ). Their overlapped spectra provide a precondition of FRET between CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs. But both of them have positive charges, so it is difficult to carry out FRET because of charge repulsion. However, HA has negative charges. So the distance between CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs can be shorted by HA as the centre，which will generate FRET phenomenon. In order to get a highly sensitive platform, it's necessary to search the proper ratios of CPNs-NH2, AuNRs and HA.

Firstly, simple mixture of CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs was investigated. Fig. 3a displayed a slight fluorescence decrease of CPNs-NH2 with the addition of AuNRs. From 0.22 μM to 0.44 μM, the fluorescence intensity appeared a tiny “plateau” and decreased by 7% (Fig. 3a-inset), indicating that the system reached a temporary balance. Continue to increase the concentration of AuNRs, the fluorescence intensity continuously declined. When the concentration of AuNRs reached 2 μM, the fluorescence intensity felled significantly by 24%. In order to explore the reasons for the decrease of fluorescence, we tested the fluorescence lifetime of CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs. The details of the specific experiment are described in the supporting information. When three typical concentrations of AuNRs (0.22 μM; 0.44 μM; 2 μM) were added to CPNs-NH2, the fluorescence lifetime did not change significantly (Fig. 1b). It can be judged that there is no energy transfer process between them. Therefore, it is inferred that there is an internal filtering effect of fluorescence (IEF) effect [26], resulting in the decrease of fluorescence.

Before three substances mixed, we explored the effect of HA to the fluorescence of CPNs-NH2. HA solutions of different concentrations (0–5.5 μg/mL) were added into CPNs-NH2 solution (1 μg/mL). Fig. 3b showed that HA has no significant effect on the fluorescence of CPNs-NH2. As a simple linear macromolecule, HA has no obvious effect on the conjugated structure of CPNs-NH2.

In order to find the best ratio of different compositions, we added different concentrations of HA solutions (0–5 μg/mL) to three mixtures of CPNs-NH2/AuNRs (1 μg/mL/0.22 μM, 1 μg/mL/0.44 μM, 1 μg/mL/2 μM) determined in the previous step. The fluorescence test was carried out. The results in Fig. 3c, e and g showed that the fluorescence of CPNs-NH2 would decrease with the addition of HA, then appeared a lowest point and gradually increased. The lowest point indicated that the efficiency of FRET between CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs is the largest, and it is the best proportion for different compositions. The concentrations of HA at the lowest point are 0.5 μg/mL, 1 μg/mL and 3 μg/mL, respectively. Moreover, we tested the Zeta potential with the addition of HA (Fig. 3d, f, Fig. 3h) and calculated the fluorescence quenching efficiency at the lowest point. With the addition of HA and AuNRs, the surface charge of CPNs-NH2 is neutralized to vary degrees, and the particle repulsion between similarities and ions is reduced. The result is that the three are close to each other and combine to form larger aggregates, that is, to form nanoplatform. The relative data were summarized in Table 1 . One can see that the charges transfer from positive to negative with the addition of HA, and the fluorescence quenching degree is greatest at the isoelectric point because the FRET efficiency reached the peak. In addition, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of three systems, (CPNs-NH2/HA/AuNRs: 1 μg/mL/0.5 μg/mL/0.22 μM; 1 μg/mL/1 μg/mL/0.44 μM; 1 μg/mL/3 μg/mL/2 μM) was different. The quenching degree of the last two systems is almost the same (81.9% and 81.6%).

Next, the fluorescence lifetime of three systems was tested. When the concentration of AuNRs was 0.22 μM, the fluorescence lifetime of this system decreased significantly. After increasing to 0.44 μM, the fluorescence lifetime of the system continued to decline. After the concentration reached 2 μM, the fluorescence lifetime did not continue to decline. This proves that it is the connection of HA that enables CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs to effectively FRET and establish nanoplatform. Furthermore, the self-assembly process of the “turn off” platform was studied by TEM. As can be seen from Fig. (2h; 2i; 2j), when HA increases to 1 μg/mL (Fig. 2j), the close binding between the components was obvious. Combined with fluorescence spectrum, Zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime test, so the second system (CPNs-NH2/HA/AuNRs: 1 μg/mL/1 μg/mL/0.44 μM) was selected to be the “turn off” platform and carried out the following experiments.

The kinetic curve of reaction time between nanoplatform and HAase was studied by measuring the change of fluorescence intensity in the system. Two different concentrations (20 U/mL; 45 U/mL) of HAase were selected for the experiment (Fig. S1). The fluorescence intensity of the two systems gradually increased with time and remained unchanged after 15 min. Therefore, a reaction time of 20 min was selected for the following tests. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence changes of CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs with different concentrations of HAase (0–75 U/mL) were studied. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of CPNs-NH2 was recovered with the increased concentrations of HAase. At 70 U/mL of HAase, the fluorescence of the platform was restored to the maximum value (85%) (Fig. 4a-inset). After linear fitting, the concentration of HAase and fluorescence intensity has a good linear relationship in the range of 1–45 U/mL (Fig. 4b). The equation as follows:
F 1 − F 0 = 7.25 X + 234.02
(
R 2
= 0.9931 )
Where [F1–F0] is the difference of fluorescence intensity of nanoplatform in the presence and absence of different concentrations of HAase, and X means the adding concentration of HAase. The detection of limit (LOD) was calculated to be 0.003 U/mL by the below formula: [27,28]
LOD =
3 S b
K
Where Sb is the standard deviation and K represents the slope between fluorescence intensity and HAase concentration.

Compared with other fluorescent materials for HAase detection, Table 2 showed the nanoplatform has significant advantages in the detection range and LOD [2,8,9,12,29,30]. This proves that the designed fluorescence platform of CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs has an excellent sensitivity to HAase.

In order to prove the specificity of our designed fluorescence platform for HAase detection, we selected different interfering substances (NaCl, KCl, glutathione (GSH), glucose (Glu), lysozyme, glucose oxidase (GOD), horseradish peroxide (HRP)), Bovine serum albumin (BSA). Multiple experiments proved that the fluorescence of CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs can be recovered only in the presence of HAase (Fig. 4c and its inset). So the nanoplatform has a good specificity for the detection of HAase.

The cytotoxicity of CPNs-NH2 and CPNs-NH2-HA/AuNRs were tested by 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) method and the details were recorded in the supporting information. The results showed that even in the presence of high concentrations of CPNs-NH2, the survival rate of HeLa cells was as high as 92% (Fig. 4d). On the other hand, in the presence of CPNs-NH2 (2 μg/mL) and HA (2 μg/mL), MTT experiments were conducted with AuNRs of different concentrations to verify the toxicity of fluorescence platform. When the concentration of AuNRs increased to 2.5 μM, the survival rate still reached 89% (Fig. 4e). This proved that a nanoplatform with good biocompatibility and low toxicity has been designed.

The real value of nanomaterials is that they can be applied to actual life rapidly. So, CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs were applied to the actual biological environment (mouse serum and plasma, human serum and plasma). The mouse serum and plasma purchased do not contain basically HAase because of the pretreatment, so the recovery experiments were carried out. Several groups of experiments revealed that the good recovery was got between 94% and 101% in Fig. 5 . Then, human serum and plasma were diluted to different samples which containing different concentrations of HAase (human serum: 5.1 U/mL and 10.4 U/mL; human plasma: 6.3 U/mL and 13.6 U/mL), and carried out the labeling experiments. The results of multiple experiments show that the recovery is between 95% and 103%, which is an ideal result. A series of labeling experiments confirmed that CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs has useful application prospect for the detection of HAase in the actual biological environment.

To summarize, a fluorescence platform composed of CPNs-NH2, HA and AuNRs was designed and carefully established for the quantitative detection of HAase. The fluorescence will be quenched because CPNs-NH2 and AuNRs can combine closely by HA through electrostatic action to actuate FRET happen, and then recovered after the addition of HAase. The reason is that HAase is a specific enzyme that degrades HA, which can cut off the process of FRET and recovery the fluorescence of CPNs-NH2. The nanoplatform of CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs was highly selective and sensitive for HAase with the wider detection range (1–45 U/mL) and lower LOD of 0.003 U/mL. Furthermore, MTT assay showed that CPNs-NH2-HA@ AuNRs had low cytotoxicity, and the nanoplatform was successfully applied to the determination of HAase in biological environment with satisfactory results. This type of fluorescence platform may supply further application advantages for the efficient detection of HAase.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are particularly grateful to the funding supporting from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 51403111, 11774188), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2020MB062,ZR2017BH053), The Project Supported by Universities Twenty Foundational Items of Jinan City (No. 2019GXRC036) and the Joint Research Foundation (2019BSHZ0013) for Young Doctorates of Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109844.

Qiu-bo Wang: Conception and design of study; acquisition of data; analysis and/or interpretation of data; drafting the manuscript. Wen Fu: Data curation, Writing-review & editing. Rui-jian Ma: acquisition of data. Qian Lu: Conception and design of study. Zhao-e Liu: Resources, Funding acquisition. Jin-shui Yao: Writing - review & editing. Qinze Liu: Writing - review & editing. Xian Zhang: Resources, Funding acquisition; revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content. Hui Zhao: Resources, Funding acquisition.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hyaluronic acid-based activatable nanomaterials for stimuli-responsive imaging and therapeutics: beyond CD44-mediated drug delivery
Adv Mater

Nano Lett

Actuators B. 2018, 275, 409-414.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modulation of assembly and disassembly of a new tetraphenylethene based nanosensor for highly selective detection of hyaluronidase
